ecoreview 0.1.41

Accuracy Metrics

Source: vignettes/accuracy.Rmd
accuracy.Rmd

After verifying documents, ecoreview can calculate how accurately ecoextract found and filled in your records. This guide explains what the metrics mean and how to use them.

Accessing the Accuracy Modal

Click the Accuracy button (bar chart icon) in the header. The modal shows metrics calculated across all verified documents in the connected database.

Metrics are only meaningful for verified documents (those with a reviewed_at timestamp). Unverified documents have unknown ground truth and are excluded.

What Gets Measured

The accuracy calculation compares the LLM’s original output against the human-corrected ground truth for each verified document.

Three things can happen to a record during review:

Event Meaning
Record kept unedited LLM extracted it correctly
Record edited LLM found it but got one or more fields wrong
Record deleted LLM hallucinated a record that doesn’t exist
Record added by reviewer LLM missed a real record

Metrics

Record Detection

Recall – what fraction of real records did the LLM find?

Recall=LLM records kept or editedLLM records kept or edited+records added by reviewer\text{Recall} = \frac{\text{LLM records kept or edited}}{\text{LLM records kept or edited} + \text{records added by reviewer}}

A low recall means the LLM is missing records that are present in the papers.

Precision – what fraction of LLM records were real?

Precision=LLM records kept or editedLLM records kept or edited+records deleted by reviewer\text{Precision} = \frac{\text{LLM records kept or edited}}{\text{LLM records kept or edited} + \text{records deleted by reviewer}}

A low precision means the LLM is hallucinating records that don’t exist.

F1 – harmonic mean of precision and recall. A single summary of detection performance.

Field-Level Accuracy

Record detection only tells half the story – a found record may still have incorrect field values. Field-level metrics measure how accurately individual fields were filled in.

Field precision – across all non-deleted, LLM-extracted records, what fraction of field values are correct (unedited)?

Major edit rate – what fraction of records required at least one edit to a “major” field (fields that uniquely identify a record in your schema, or required fields)?

Average edits per document – how many field corrections were needed per verified document on average.

Interpreting Results

Metric Healthy range Concern
Recall > 0.90 LLM missing real records
Precision > 0.90 LLM hallucinating records
Field precision > 0.85 Fields frequently wrong
Major edit rate < 0.15 Core fields often incorrect
Avg edits/doc < 3 Many corrections needed per paper

These ranges are rough guides – the right threshold depends on your schema complexity and domain.

Programmatic Access

Accuracy metrics are also available via ecoextract::calculate_accuracy():

accuracy <- ecoextract::calculate_accuracy("ecoextract_records.db")

# Detection metrics
accuracy$detection_recall
accuracy$detection_precision
accuracy$detection_f1

# Field-level metrics
accuracy$field_precision
accuracy$major_edit_rate
accuracy$avg_edits_per_document

# Per-document breakdown
accuracy$by_document

For a detailed explanation of how each metric is derived, see ACCURACY.md in the ecoextract repository.

Using Accuracy to Improve Extraction

Accuracy metrics guide prompt and schema iteration:

Low recall → the LLM is missing records. Review the extraction prompt – add examples, clarify what counts as a record, check if papers with many records have lower recall than those with few.

Low precision → the LLM is hallucinating. Tighten the schema’s required fields and add negative examples to the prompt (“do not extract X if Y is absent”).

Low field precision on specific columns → those fields are ambiguous in the prompt or schema. Add a definition, examples, and edge-case guidance for those fields.

High major edit rate → the unique/required fields are frequently wrong. These are the most impactful errors – prioritise fixing them in the prompt.

The typical iteration loop:

  1. Process a small batch (10-20 papers)
  2. Review in ecoreview, click Verify for each
  3. Check the Accuracy modal
  4. Update ecoextract/extraction_prompt.md or ecoextract/schema.json
  5. Force re-extract the same batch and compare metrics
  6. When satisfied, process the full corpus

Exporting for Analysis

Use Export Records CSV to get a flat file suitable for further analysis in R or Excel. The export includes:

  • All non-deleted records joined with document metadata
  • record_id for tracing back to the database
  • reviewed_at on each row (from the document join) to filter to verified records
library(readr)
library(dplyr)

records <- read_csv("myproject_records_20260706.csv")

# Verified documents only
verified <- records |> filter(!is.na(reviewed_at))

# Quick summary
cat("Documents verified:", n_distinct(verified$document_id), "\n")
cat("Total records:", nrow(verified), "\n")

On this page

Developed by Nathan Layman.

list(text = “EcoReview - Human-in-the-loop review for ecological data extraction”)
